title: The Amend Kata
subtitle: One commit, three mistakes, zero excuses

briefing.narrative:
You just made your first commit in the monastery's new project. You feel proud. You lean back. You sip your herbal tea.

Then you notice three things at once:
1. The commit message says "Intial setup." INTIAL.
2. You forgot to create the readme.md the Head Monk specifically asked for.
3. There's a console.log('debug') sitting in app.js like a spiritual blemish.

The Head Monk hasn't pulled yet. There's still time. You can fix all three problems in ONE amend. The ancient texts call this "The Amend Kata" -- correcting your mistakes before anyone notices.

Breathe. Focus. Amend.
briefing.concept: git commit --amend is more powerful than just fixing a message. You can also add new files and change existing ones -- all folded into the same commit as if the mistake never happened. Stage your changes first, then amend.
objectives: Fix the debug line in app.js
Amend the commit to fix the message, include readme.md, and update app.js

hints.text: The monastery cat stretches on a scroll that reads: 'First fix the files and stage them with git add. THEN amend.'
A monk whispers: 'Create readme.md, edit app.js to remove the debug line, git add both files, then git commit --amend -m "Initial setup".'
The cat does it for you: echo '# Readme' > readme.md && edit app.js to remove the debug line && git add app.js readme.md && git commit --amend -m "Initial setup"
